title: Magnesium Deficiency Risk Assessment
description: Take our free magnesium deficiency quiz. Check for symptoms like muscle cramps, insomnia, anxiety, and migraines in this science-based self-assessment tool.
scientificBasis: Based on clinical hypomagnesemia symptoms documented in the Journal of the American College of Nutrition and subclinical magnesium deficiency criteria (serum Mg below 0.85 mmol/L). Estimated 50–80% of the population may have suboptimal intake per DiNicolantonio et al. (2018), Open Heart.
seoIntro:
Magnesium is involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions in your body, making it one of the most critical minerals for overall health. It plays essential roles in muscle and nerve function, blood sugar regulation, blood pressure management, and protein synthesis. Despite its importance, magnesium deficiency is remarkably common — some researchers estimate that up to 80% of adults may not be getting enough.

Subclinical magnesium deficiency — where levels are low enough to cause symptoms but not low enough to show on standard blood tests — is particularly insidious. Common symptoms include muscle cramps and twitches (especially at night), difficulty sleeping, anxiety, heart palpitations, headaches and migraines, constipation, and fatigue.

Several lifestyle factors increase your risk: high stress levels (stress depletes magnesium), alcohol consumption, certain medications (diuretics, PPIs), and a diet low in nuts, seeds, leafy greens, and whole grains. Chronic conditions like type 2 diabetes also increase magnesium requirements.

This assessment evaluates your symptoms and risk factors against clinical criteria for magnesium insufficiency. Standard serum magnesium tests catch only severe deficiency — an RBC (red blood cell) magnesium test is more sensitive for detecting subclinical deficiency.

questions.text: Do you experience muscle cramps, twitches, or spasms?
Do you experience difficulty falling or staying asleep?
How often do you feel anxious or experience heart palpitations?
How much alcohol do you consume per week?
Do you consume foods rich in magnesium (dark leafy greens, nuts, seeds, dark chocolate, whole grains) regularly?
Do you take medications that deplete magnesium (diuretics, PPIs, certain antibiotics)?
Do you experience frequent headaches or migraines?
How would you rate your overall stress level?
Do you have or are you at risk for type 2 diabetes?

results.title: Low Risk — Magnesium Status Likely Adequate
results.description: No significant indicators of magnesium deficiency. Your diet and lifestyle suggest adequate magnesium levels.

results.title: Mild Risk — Suboptimal Magnesium Possible
results.description: Some risk factors present. Consider increasing magnesium-rich foods in your diet.
recommendations: Increase magnesium-rich foods: dark leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains
Consider a magnesium supplement (200–400 mg/day of magnesium glycinate or citrate)

results.title: Moderate Risk — Possible Magnesium Insufficiency
results.description: Multiple indicators of possible hypomagnesemia. A serum magnesium test (and ideally RBC magnesium) is recommended.
recommendations: Request both serum and RBC magnesium blood tests
Discuss symptoms with your healthcare provider
Consider magnesium supplementation under medical guidance
Review medications that may deplete magnesium with your prescriber

results.title: High Risk — Possible Magnesium Deficiency
results.description: Strong risk profile for magnesium deficiency. Medical evaluation and guided supplementation recommended.
recommendations: See your healthcare provider for comprehensive testing
Request RBC magnesium test (more accurate than serum)
Address contributing factors (stress, medications, alcohol, diet)

faq.question: Why is magnesium deficiency so common?
faq.answer: Modern diets are often low in magnesium due to soil depletion, processed food consumption, and reduced intake of nuts, seeds, and leafy greens. Additionally, stress, alcohol, caffeine, and certain medications all deplete magnesium stores.

faq.question: What is the best form of magnesium supplement?
faq.answer: Magnesium glycinate is well-absorbed and gentle on the stomach, making it ideal for correcting deficiency. Magnesium citrate is also well-absorbed and can help with constipation. Magnesium oxide is poorly absorbed and primarily acts as a laxative. Magnesium threonate may cross the blood-brain barrier and benefit cognitive function.

faq.question: Can a standard blood test detect magnesium deficiency?
faq.answer: Standard serum magnesium tests only detect severe deficiency because less than 1% of your body's magnesium is in the blood. An RBC (red blood cell) magnesium test is more sensitive for detecting subclinical deficiency, which is far more common.

faq.question: How much magnesium do I need daily?
faq.answer: The RDA is 310–320 mg for women and 400–420 mg for men. Many experts suggest optimal intake may be higher. Good sources include pumpkin seeds, almonds, spinach, dark chocolate, avocados, and black beans.

sources: DiNicolantonio, J.J. et al. (2018). Subclinical magnesium deficiency: a principal driver of cardiovascular disease. Open Heart, 5(1), e000668.
Guerrera, M.P. et al. (2009). Therapeutic uses of magnesium. American Family Physician, 80(2), 157-162.
National Institutes of Health. Office of Dietary Supplements — Magnesium Fact Sheet.
